agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes reducing delivery ETA granularity from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to a core customer-facing promise in the logistics domain. ETA precision directly affects customer planning, operational accountability, and competitive positioning.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Shipment Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the projected delivery time communicated to the recipient. In logistics, this is a customer-facing promise that drives planning behavior."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which a delivery is expected. Can range from exact timestamp to multi-day range. Narrower windows imply higher operational precision."},{"term":"SLA (Service Level Agreement)","definition":"Contractual commitment on delivery performance, often tied to ETA accuracy. Violations may trigger penalties or credits."},{"term":"Last-Mile Delivery","definition":"The final leg of the delivery journey from distribution hub to recipient. Most variable and hardest to predict accurately."},{"term":"On-Time Delivery Rate","definition":"Percentage of deliveries completed within the promised ETA window. A key operational KPI that directly correlates with customer satisfaction."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every shipment must display an ETA to the recipient from the moment it enters the delivery pipeline.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment without a visible ETA is invisible to the customer, violating the fundamental promise of delivery tracking.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment in any active status, when a recipient views tracking, then an ETA is displayed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"ETA format must be consistent across all customer-facing channels (app, email, SMS, web tracking).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Inconsistent ETA formats across channels erode trust and cause confusion — a customer seeing '14:30 Friday' in email but 'Friday' on the web would question accuracy.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment, when the ETA is displayed on any channel, then the format and granularity are identical across all channels."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"ETA changes must be communicated to the recipient when the delivery date shifts.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Notification policy is a business preference, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to only notify on delays beyond a threshold.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment whose ETA date has changed, when the change is detected, then the recipient is notified."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"If contractual SLAs define time-level precision, ETA display must meet or exceed that precision.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Displaying a date-only ETA when the SLA promises a time-window delivery would misrepresent the service contract and create legal exposure.","testable_assertion":"Given a shipment under an SLA with time-level precision, when ETA is displayed, then it includes at least the precision defined in the SLA."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A shipment is in transit with a calculated ETA","when":"The recipient views the tracking page","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') rather than an exact timestamp"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A shipment is covered by an SLA that specifies time-window delivery guarantees","when":"The ETA is rendered for that shipment","then":"The ETA retains sufficient precision to honor the SLA commitment (not downgraded to date-only)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A shipment's ETA changes such that the delivery DATE shifts","when":"The system detects the date change","then":"The recipient is notified of the updated delivery date"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The ETA format is changed to date-only","when":"The ETA is displayed across email, SMS, app, and web tracking","then":"All channels consistently show the date-only format"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Internal operations dashboards used by dispatchers and support agents","when":"They view shipment ETAs","then":"They still have access to time-level granularity for operational decision-making, regardless of customer-facing format changes"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Same-day delivery: If the ETA is today and format is date-only, the customer has no idea if delivery is in 30 minutes or 8 hours.","expected_behavior":"Same-day deliveries should retain time-level or time-window granularity (e.g., 'Today by 5 PM') since date-only provides zero useful planning information."},{"scenario":"Multi-day delivery windows spanning weekends: 'Delivery by Friday' when the package arrives Saturday.","expected_behavior":"If the carrier delivers on weekends, the date must reflect the actual expected date. If not, the system must account for non-delivery days."},{"scenario":"Time-sensitive shipments (medical, perishable, legal documents): Date-only removes critical planning precision.","expected_behavior":"Shipment types with time-sensitivity should be exempt from date-only formatting and retain time-level ETAs."},{"scenario":"Timezone ambiguity: 'Delivery by Friday' — whose Friday? Sender's timezone or recipient's?","expected_behavior":"Date-only ETAs must resolve to the recipient's local timezone to avoid a delivery on Thursday night (sender's time) surprising a recipient who expected Friday."},{"scenario":"ETA that falls on a holiday: 'Delivery by December 25' when no deliveries occur on that day.","expected_behavior":"System must validate ETA dates against delivery calendars and adjust to next valid delivery date."},{"scenario":"B2B customers with receiving dock hours who currently plan staffing around time-level ETAs.","expected_behavior":"B2B recipients with dock scheduling needs must either retain time-level ETAs or be given an opt-in to higher precision."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reducing ETA precision lowers the bar for on-time delivery metrics — fewer 'late' deliveries when the window is a full day instead of a specific time. This may reduce SLA penalty payouts short-term but could mask operational inefficiencies. If premium/express tiers lose time-level ETAs, customers paying for premium service may downgrade or churn, reducing revenue from high-margin service tiers. Potential savings from reduced customer support volume if fewer 'where is my package' calls are triggered by missed hourly ETAs.","operational":"Significant reduction in operational pressure on last-mile logistics. Time-level ETAs require real-time route optimization and dynamic recalculation; date-only ETAs reduce computational and coordination overhead. However, internal operations (dispatch, routing, support) still need time-level data — this change must be purely a presentation-layer decision, not a removal of time-level calculation from the pipeline. Risk of operational complacency if teams optimize against the looser date-only standard.","user":"This is the highest-impact dimension. Customers who currently plan their day around 'delivery by 14:30' will lose that ability. For residential recipients who must be home to receive packages, this forces them to block an entire day instead of a time slot — a significant user experience regression. Customer satisfaction surveys in logistics consistently show ETA precision as a top-3 driver of satisfaction. Competitors offering time-level windows (Amazon, UPS, FedEx all provide time windows) would gain a differentiation advantage.","risk":"Legal/contractual risk if existing SLAs or service tier descriptions promise time-level ETAs. Reputational risk from perceived service downgrade. Competitive risk as the industry trend is toward MORE precision (not less). Data integrity risk if the underlying time-level calculation is removed rather than just the display — this would be irreversible and prevent future re-introduction of precise ETAs.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Recipients (Residential)","impact":"Must block entire day for delivery instead of a specific time window. Significant inconvenience, especially for signature-required or high-value deliveries."},{"group":"End Recipients (B2B / Commercial)","impact":"Cannot schedule dock workers, forklifts, or receiving staff around a date-only ETA. May require maintaining separate communication channel with carrier for time-level coordination."},{"group":"Customer Support Teams","impact":"Mixed impact. Fewer complaints about missed exact-time ETAs, but more 'when exactly will it arrive today?' inquiries since the date-only ETA provides insufficient information."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must retain access to time-level data internally. If the change removes time-level calculation (not just display), dispatch loses critical routing optimization data."},{"group":"Sales / Account Management","impact":"Enterprise customers may perceive this as a service downgrade. Account managers need talking points and possibly contractual amendments for SLA-bound accounts."},{"group":"Product / Competitive Strategy","impact":"Moves against the industry trend of increasing delivery precision. May need to justify to leadership why the company is reducing a capability competitors are investing in."},{"group":"Premium / Express Service Tier Customers","impact":"Customers paying a premium for faster, more predictable delivery lose a key value differentiator. High churn risk in this high-margin segment."}]},"confidence":{"level":50,"reasoning":"The proposal lacks critical context: the business motivation for this change, whether existing SLAs include time-level commitments, what service tiers exist and their contractual obligations, current ETA accuracy rates (is this change motivated by poor accuracy?), and whether this is a display-only change or a removal of time-level calculation entirely. Without these, the analysis is necessarily speculative on several high-impact dimensions.","high_confidence_areas":["User experience regression for recipients who plan around time-level ETAs","Need to exempt same-day and time-sensitive deliveries from date-only format","Requirement to maintain internal operational access to time-level data","Cross-channel consistency requirement"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether existing SLAs contractually require time-level precision","Whether this is motivated by poor ETA accuracy (which would change the cost-benefit calculus significantly)","Whether service tiers exist that promise time-level delivery windows","The actual financial impact — depends entirely on customer segment mix and contract terms","Whether this is display-only or removes time-level calculation from the pipeline"],"assumptions":["This is a customer-facing display change, not a removal of time-level calculation from the backend","The system currently has multiple customer-facing channels that all show ETAs","There may be different service tiers with different precision expectations","The underlying logistics pipeline will continue to calculate time-level ETAs for operational use"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"Business motivation for the change was never stated. Is this driven by poor ETA accuracy (showing exact times that are frequently wrong), cost reduction, or simplification? The motivation fundamentally changes whether this is a pragmatic improvement or a capability regression.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Engineering","All recipient segments"],"options":["If motivated by poor accuracy: fix accuracy rather than hiding precision","If motivated by cost: quantify savings vs. churn risk","If motivated by simplification: consider tiered approach (date-only for standard, time-level for premium)"],"recommendation":"Provide the business rationale before proceeding. If accuracy is the driver, consider showing time-windows (e.g., 'morning/afternoon') as a middle ground rather than jumping to date-only."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Existing SLA and contractual obligations with time-level delivery guarantees were never provided. If contracts promise time-window delivery, this change may breach those agreements.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales","Account Management","Legal","Enterprise Customers"],"options":["Audit all active SLAs for time-level precision commitments","Exempt SLA-bound shipments from the format change","Renegotiate affected contracts before rollout"],"recommendation":"Legal and Sales must audit existing contracts for time-level commitments before any implementation begins. BR-4 is an invariant — violating it creates legal exposure."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Service tier structure and premium delivery offerings were never described. Premium tiers that promise time-level precision cannot be downgraded to date-only without destroying their value proposition.","affected_stakeholders":["Premium Customers","Sales","Product"],"options":["Apply date-only only to standard tier","Retain time-level for all premium/express tiers","Introduce configurable precision per service tier"],"recommendation":"Map all service tiers and their ETA precision promises before scoping this change. Premium tiers must retain time-level ETAs."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"It is unclear whether this change is display-only (backend still calculates time-level ETAs) or a deeper architectural change that removes time-level calculation. The latter would be far more impactful and harder to reverse.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","Dispatch"],"options":["Display-only change: low risk, reversible, operations unaffected","Calculation removal: high risk, irreversible, operations degraded"],"recommendation":"Confirm this is strictly a presentation-layer change. Time-level calculation must be preserved for internal operations and potential future re-introduction of precise customer ETAs."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this delivery ETA change proposal from a domain perspective.

This is a logistics domain question about changing ETA precision from exact timestamps to date-only windows. Let me think through this carefully.

Classification: This is an `ambiguity` scenario. The proposal is vague - "date-only windows" could mean several things, and the business rationale isn't stated. There are multiple valid interpretations and missing clarity on what problem this solves.

Wait - let me reconsider. The proposal itself is stated clearly enough. But critical information is missing: WHY are they making this change? What's the current accuracy rate? Are there SLA implications? What do customers expect?


Actually, I realize the real issue here is that nobody's provided any of the foundational context - no SLA details, customer expectations, contractual terms, accuracy metrics, or business justification. This is a `missing_context` problem, and I need to work with what's absent rather than what's stated.
